Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).

8 9 # cuda-oxide 10 11 cuda-oxide is a custom rustc backend for compiling GPU kernels in pure Rust. 12 The workspace combines: 13 14 - single-source compilation -- host and device code live in the same file, built with one `cargo oxide build` 15 - a rustc codegen backend that compiles `#[kernel]` functions to CUDA PTX 16 - device-side abstractions (type-safe indexing, shared memory, scoped atomics, barriers, TMA, warp/cluster ops) 17 - a host-side runtime for memory management, pinned host transfers, and kernel launching (`cuda-core`, `cuda-async`) 18 - a rust-native compilation pipeline using [Pliron](https://github.com/vaivaswatha/pliron), an MLIR-like IR framework in Rust (Rust → Rust MIR → Pliron IR → LLVM IR → PTX) 19 20 ## Project Status 21 22 cuda-oxide is an experimental compiler that demonstrates how CUDA SIMT kernels can be written natively in pure Rust -- no DSLs, no foreign language bindings -- and made available to the broader Rust community. The project is in an early stage (alpha) and under active development: you should expect bugs, incomplete features, and API breakage as we work to improve it. That said, we hope you'll try it in your own work and help shape its direction by sharing feedback on your experience. 23 24 Please see [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md) if you're interested in contributing to the project. 25 26 ## Quick Start 27 28 ```rust 29 use cuda_device::{cuda_module, kernel, thread, DisjointSlice}; 30 use cuda_core::{CudaContext, DeviceBuffer, LaunchConfig}; 31 32 // Device: generic kernel that applies any function to each element. 33 // F can be a closure with captures — rustc monomorphizes it to a concrete type. 34 #[cuda_module] 35 mod kernels { 36 use super::*; 37 38 #[kernel] 39 pub fn map T + Copy>(f: F, input: &[T], mut out: DisjointSlice) { 40 let idx = thread::index_1d(); 41 let i = idx.get(); 42 if let Some(out_elem) = out.get_mut(idx) { 43 *out_elem = f(input[i]); 44 } 45 } 46 } 47 48 fn main() { 49 let ctx = CudaContext::new(0).unwrap(); 50 let stream = ctx.default_stream(); 51 52 let data: Vec = (0..1024).map(|i| i as f32).collect(); 53 let input = DeviceBuffer::from_host(&stream, &data).unwrap(); 54 let mut output = DeviceBuffer::::zeroed(&stream, 1024).unwrap(); 55 56 let module = kernels::load(&ctx).unwrap(); 57 58 // Launch with a closure — factor is captured and passed to the GPU automatically 59 let factor = 2.5f32; 60 module 61 .map::( 62 &stream, 63 LaunchConfig::for_num_elems(1024), 64 move |x: f32| x * factor, 65 &input, 66 &mut output, 67 ) 68 .unwrap(); 69 70 let result = output.to_host_vec(&stream).unwrap(); 71 assert!((result[1] - 2.5).abs() < 1e-5); 72 } 73 ``` 74 75 The above example defines a generic `#[kernel]` function `map` that accepts any 76 `Fn(T) -> T` closure. `#[cuda_module]` embeds the generated device artifact into 77 the host binary and generates a typed `module.map::(...)` launch method. 78 The closure `move |x| x * factor` is captured, scalarized, and passed as kernel 79 parameters automatically. 80 81 For composable async GPU work, `stream:` disappears, `{kernel}_async` returns a 82 lazy `DeviceOperation`, and execution happens when you call `.sync()` or 83 `.await`. 84 85 ```rust 86 use cuda_async::device_operation::DeviceOperation; 87 88 // Assuming `module`, `input`, and `output` come from the cuda-async setup: 89 let factor = 2.5f32; 90 module 91 .map_async::( 92 LaunchConfig::for_num_elems(1024), 93 move |x: f32| x * factor, 94 &input, 95 &mut output, 96 )? 97 .sync()?; 98 // or: .await?; 99 ``` 100 101 See the `async_mlp` example and `crates/cuda-async/README.md` for the full async setup. 102 103 ```bash 104 # Build and run an example 105 cargo oxide run host_closure 106 107 # Show full compilation pipeline (Rust MIR → dialect-mir → mem2reg → LLVM dialect → LLVM IR → PTX) 108 cargo oxide pipeline vecadd 109 110 # Debug with cuda-gdb 111 cargo oxide debug vecadd --tui 112 ``` 113 114 ## Setup 115 116 ### Requirements 117 118 - **cargo-oxide** — cargo subcommand that drives the build pipeline (`cargo oxide run`, `build`, `debug`, etc.) 119 - **Rust nightly** with `rust-src` and `rustc-dev` and `llvm-tools` components (pinned in `rust-toolchain.toml`) 120 - **CUDA Toolkit** (12.x+) 121 - **Clang + libclang dev headers** (`clang-21` / `libclang-common-21-dev`) — needed by `bindgen` when building the host `cuda-bindings` crate 122 - **Linux** (tested on Ubuntu 24.04) 123 124 ### Install 125 126 #### cargo-oxide 127 128 Inside the cuda-oxide repo, `cargo oxide` works out of the box via a workspace alias. 129 130 For use outside the repo (your own projects), install it with the pinned nightly toolchain: 131 132 ```bash 133 cargo +nightly-2026-04-03 install --git https://github.com/NVlabs/cuda-oxide.git cargo-oxide 134 ``` 135 136 On first run, `cargo-oxide` will automatically fetch and build the codegen backend. 137 138 #### Nix (alternative) 139 140 If you have Nix with flakes enabled, `nix develop` in the repo gives you a reproducible shell with CUDA 13, LLVM 22, Clang, and the pinned Rust nightly — no manual apt installs. The shellHook auto-discovers host NVIDIA drivers on NixOS and non-NixOS systems. 141 142 ```bash 143 nix develop # full dev shell in this repo 144 nix run github:NVlabs/cuda-oxide#new my-project # bootstrap a project 145 ``` 146 147 #### Rust 148 149 ```bash 150 # Toolchain installed automatically via rust-toolchain.toml 151 # Manual install if needed: 152 rustup toolchain install nightly-2026-04-03 153 rustup component add rust-src rustc-dev --toolchain nightly-2026-04-03 154 ``` 155 156 #### CUDA 157 158 ```bash 159 export PATH="/usr/local/cuda/bin:$PATH" 160 nvcc --version 161 ``` 162 163 #### LLVM (optional) 164 165 ```bash 166 # Ubuntu/Debian 167 sudo apt install llvm-21 168 ``` 169 170 If your distro packages do not provide `llvm-21`, use LLVM's apt helper: 171 172 ```bash 173 sudo apt-get install -y lsb-release wget software-properties-common gnupg 174 wget https://apt.llvm.org/llvm.sh && chmod +x llvm.sh 175 sudo ./llvm.sh 21 176 ``` 177 178 ```bash 179 # Verify NVPTX support 180 llc-21 --version | grep nvptx 181 ``` 182 183 The pipeline prefers `llc` in Rust toolchain, and auto-discovers `llc-22` and `llc-21` on `PATH` (in that order). 184 To pin a specific binary, set `CUDA_OXIDE_LLC=/usr/bin/llc-21`. 185 186 > We emit TMA / tcgen05 / WGMMA intrinsics that `llc` from LLVM 20 and earlier can't handle. 187 > Simple kernels might still work with an older `llc`, but anything Hopper / Blackwell needs 21+. 188 189 #### Clang (host `cuda-bindings`) 190 191 The host `cuda-bindings` crate runs `bindgen`, which loads libclang and needs 192 clang's own resource-dir `stddef.h` — a bare `libclang1-*` runtime is not 193 enough. 194 195 ```bash 196 sudo apt install clang-21 # or libclang-common-21-dev 197 ``` 198 199 `cargo oxide doctor` catches this up front; the symptom otherwise is a cryptic 200 `'stddef.h' file not found` during the host build. 201 202 #### Dev Container 203 204 The repository includes a standard devcontainer setup in `.devcontainer/` for a 205 reproducible CUDA, LLVM, Clang, and Rust environment. See the 206 [installation chapter](cuda-oxide-book/getting-started/installation.md#dev-container) 207 for editor and CLI usage. 208 209 ### Verifying Installation 210 211 ```bash 212 # Check that all prerequisites are in place 213 cargo oxide doctor 214 215 # Build and run an example end-to-end 216 cargo oxide run vecadd 217 ``` 218 219 `cargo oxide doctor` validates your Rust toolchain, CUDA toolkit, LLVM, and 220 codegen backend. If everything is configured correctly, `cargo oxide run vecadd` 221 compiles a Rust kernel to PTX, launches it on the GPU, and prints 222 `✓ SUCCESS: All 1024 elements correct!`. 223 224 ## Examples 225 226 **60+ examples** in `crates/rustc-codegen-cuda/examples/`. Highlights: 227 228 | Example | Description | 229 |----------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------| 230 | `vecadd` | Vector addition -- canonical first example | 231 | `host_closure` | Generic kernels with closures passed from host | 232 | `generic` | Generic kernels with monomorphization (`scale`) | 233 | `ord_cmp` | Device-side `Ord::cmp` lowering for signed and unsigned integers | 234 | `gemm_sol_final` | Canonical Blackwell GEMM SoL: size-specialized CLC + cg2 + vector stores | 235 | `gemm_sol` | Historical GEMM kernel progression and comparison kernels | 236 | `tcgen05` | Blackwell tensor cores (sm_100a): TMEM, MMA, cta_group::2 | 237 | `atomics` | GPU atomics: 6 types x 3 scopes x 5 orderings (20 tests) | 238 | `atomic_f16` | Scalar f16 atomics: per-scope correctness checks + f32 vs f16 bench | 239 | `cluster` | Thread Block Clusters + DSMEM ring exchange (Hopper+) | 240 | `async_mlp` | Async MLP pipeline: GEMM → MatVec → ReLU across concurrent streams | 241 | `mathdx_ffi_test` | cuFFTDx thread-level FFT + cuBLASDx block-level GEMM | 242 | `device_ffi_test` | Device FFI: Rust kernels calling C++ CCCL warp-level reductions via LTOIR| 243 | `async_vecadd` | Async GPU execution with `cuda-async` and `DeviceOperation` | 244 | `cross_crate_kernel` | Library crates defining kernels, bundled into binaries | 245 | `cuda_module_in_lib` | `#[cuda_module]` in a library crate, loaded by embedded bundle name | 246 247 ```bash 248 cargo oxide run vecadd 249 cargo oxide run gemm_sol_final 250 ``` 251 252 ## Crate Overview 253 254 ### User-Facing Crates 255 256 | Crate | Description | 257 |---------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------| 258 | `cuda-device` | Device intrinsics (`thread::*`, `warp::*`, barriers) | 259 | `cuda-host` | Typed module loading, launch helpers, LTOIR loader | 260 | `cuda-macros` | Proc macros (`#[cuda_module]`, `#[kernel]`, `gpu_printf!`) | 261 | `cuda-bindings` | Raw `bindgen` FFI bindings to `cuda.h` | 262 | `cuda-core` | Safe RAII wrappers (`CudaContext`, `CudaStream`, `DeviceBuffer`, ...) | 263 | `cuda-async` | Async execution layer (`DeviceOperation`, `DeviceFuture`, `DeviceBox`) | 264 | `libnvvm-sys` | `dlopen` bindings to libNVVM (used by `cuda-host::ltoir`) | 265 | `nvjitlink-sys` | `dlopen` bindings to nvJitLink (used by `cuda-host::ltoir`) | 266 267 ### Compiler Crates 268 269 | Crate | Description | 270 |----------------------|-------------------------------------------------------| 271 | `rustc-codegen-cuda` | Custom rustc backend | 272 | `mir-importer` | Rust MIR -> `dialect-mir` translation + pipeline | 273 | `mir-lower` | `dialect-mir` -> LLVM dialect lowering | 274 | `dialect-mir` | pliron dialect modelling Rust MIR | 275 | `llvm-export` | pliron-llvm shim + textual `.ll` exporter | 276 | `dialect-nvvm` | pliron dialect modelling NVVM intrinsics | 277 278 ### Build Tooling 279 280 | Crate | Description | 281 |----------------|------------------------------------------------------| 282 | `cargo-oxide` | Cargo subcommand (`cargo oxide run`, etc.) | 283 284 ### Documentation 285 286 | Directory | Description | 287 |---------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------| 288 | `cuda-oxide-book` | Project book (Sphinx + MyST) — guides, compiler internals, API ref | 289 290 ## Status 291 292 ### Highlights: 293 294 - End-to-end Rust -> PTX compilation 295 - Unified single-source compilation (host + device in one file) 296 - Generic functions with monomorphization 297 - Closures with captures (move and non-move via HMM) 298 - User-defined structs, enums, pattern matching 299 - Full GPU intrinsic support (thread, warp, shared memory, barriers, TMA, clusters, atomics) 300 - Cross-crate kernels 301 - LTOIR generation for Blackwell+ (device-side LTO) 302 - Device FFI: Rust <-> C++/CCCL interop via LTOIR 303 - MathDx integration: cuFFTDx thread-level FFT, cuBLASDx block-level GEMM 304 - Tile interop (experimental): [`cutile_inter_kernel`](crates/rustc-codegen-cuda/examples/cutile_inter_kernel/README.md) chains a cutile-rs Tile kernel and a cuda-oxide SIMT PTX kernel on the same CUDA stream over shared device tensors. Intra-kernel Tile interop is work in progress and tracked in [#96](https://github.com/NVlabs/cuda-oxide/issues/96). 305 - Host runtime: `cuda-core` (explicit control, pinned host transfers) and `cuda-async` (composable async operations) 306 - Canonical Blackwell GEMM SoL example with size-specialized M256xN256/M512xN256 CLC + cta_group::2 kernels and vectorized epilogues (see `gemm_sol_final`) 307 308 ## Documentation 309 310 **WIP:** 🚧 The **[cuda-oxide book](https://nvlabs.github.io/cuda-oxide/)** is the primary reference for the project. It covers SIMT kernel authoring in Rust, synchronous and asynchronous GPU programming, the compiler architecture, and more. 311 312 To build and serve the book locally, see [cuda-oxide-book/README.md](./cuda-oxide-book/README.md). 313 314 ## Ecosystem 315 316 cuda-oxide is one of several Rust + GPU efforts under active development. Projects in this space address different parts of the problem — Vulkan/SPIR-V for graphics, implicit offload via LLVM, third-party CUDA backends, safe driver bindings — and we've been working with maintainers across the broader Rust GPU community on how to move GPU computing in Rust forward together. For where cuda-oxide fits relative to other projects, see the [Ecosystem appendix](https://nvlabs.github.io/cuda-oxide/appendix/ecosystem.html) of the book. 317 318 ## License 319 320 The `cuda-bindings` crate is licensed under the NVIDIA Software License: [LICENSE-NVIDIA](LICENSE-NVIDIA). All other crates are licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0: [LICENSE-APACHE](LICENSE-APACHE). 321

file.content: # cuda-oxide cuda-oxide is a custom rustc backend for compiling GPU kernels in pure Rust. The workspace combines: - single-source compilation -- host and device code live in the same file, built with one `cargo oxide build` - a rustc codegen backend that compiles `#[kernel]` functions to CUDA PTX - device-side abstractions (type-safe indexing, shared memory, scoped atomics, barriers, TMA, warp/cluster ops) - a host-side runtime for memory management, pinned host transfers, and kernel launching (`cuda-core`, `cuda-async`) - a rust-native compilation pipeline using [Pliron](https://github.com/vaivaswatha/pliron), an MLIR-like IR framework in Rust (Rust → Rust MIR → Pliron IR → LLVM IR → PTX) ## Project Status cuda-oxide is an experimental compiler that demonstrates how CUDA SIMT kernels can be written natively in pure Rust -- no DSLs, no foreign language bindings -- and made available to the broader Rust community. The project is in an early stage (alpha) and under active development: you should expect bugs, incomplete features, and API breakage as we work to improve it. That said, we hope you'll try it in your own work and help shape its direction by sharing feedback on your experience. Please see [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md) if you're interested in contributing to the project. ## Quick Start ```rust use cuda_device::{cuda_module, kernel, thread, DisjointSlice}; use cuda_core::{CudaContext, DeviceBuffer, LaunchConfig}; // Device: generic kernel that applies any function to each element. // F can be a closure with captures — rustc monomorphizes it to a concrete type. #[cuda_module] mod kernels { use super::*; #[kernel] pub fn map T + Copy>(f: F, input: &[T], mut out: DisjointSlice) { let idx = thread::index_1d(); let i = idx.get(); if let Some(out_elem) = out.get_mut(idx) { *out_elem = f(input[i]); } } } fn main() { let ctx = CudaContext::new(0).unwrap(); let stream = ctx.default_stream(); let data: Vec = (0..1024).map(|i| i as f32).collect(); let input = DeviceBuffer::from_host(&stream, &data).unwrap(); let mut output = DeviceBuffer::::zeroed(&stream, 1024).unwrap(); let module = kernels::load(&ctx).unwrap(); // Launch with a closure — factor is captured and passed to the GPU automatically let factor = 2.5f32; module .map::( &stream, LaunchConfig::for_num_elems(1024), move |x: f32| x * factor, &input, &mut output, ) .unwrap(); let result = output.to_host_vec(&stream).unwrap(); assert!((result[1] - 2.5).abs() < 1e-5); } ``` The above example defines a generic `#[kernel]` function `map` that accepts any `Fn(T) -> T` closure. `#[cuda_module]` embeds the generated device artifact into the host binary and generates a typed `module.map::(...)` launch method. The closure `move |x| x * factor` is captured, scalarized, and passed as kernel parameters automatically. For composable async GPU work, `stream:` disappears, `{kernel}_async` returns a lazy `DeviceOperation`, and execution happens when you call `.sync()` or `.await`. ```rust use cuda_async::device_operation::DeviceOperation; // Assuming `module`, `input`, and `output` come from the cuda-async setup: let factor = 2.5f32; module .map_async::( LaunchConfig::for_num_elems(1024), move |x: f32| x * factor, &input, &mut output, )? .sync()?; // or: .await?; ``` See the `async_mlp` example and `crates/cuda-async/README.md` for the full async setup. ```bash # Build and run an example cargo oxide run host_closure # Show full compilation pipeline (Rust MIR → dialect-mir → mem2reg → LLVM dialect → LLVM IR → PTX) cargo oxide pipeline vecadd # Debug with cuda-gdb cargo oxide debug vecadd --tui ``` ## Setup ### Requirements - **cargo-oxide** — cargo subcommand that drives the build pipeline (`cargo oxide run`, `build`, `debug`, etc.) - **Rust nightly** with `rust-src` and `rustc-dev` and `llvm-tools` components (pinned in `rust-toolchain.toml`) - **CUDA Toolkit** (12.x+) - **Clang + libclang dev headers** (`clang-21` / `libclang-common-21-dev`) — needed by `bindgen` when building the host `cuda-bindings` crate - **Linux** (tested on Ubuntu 24.04) ### Install #### cargo-oxide Inside the cuda-oxide repo, `cargo oxide` works out of the box via a workspace alias. For use outside the repo (your own projects), install it with the pinned nightly toolchain: ```bash cargo +nightly-2026-04-03 install --git https://github.com/NVlabs/cuda-oxide.git cargo-oxide ``` On first run, `cargo-oxide` will automatically fetch and build the codegen backend. #### Nix (alternative) If you have Nix with flakes enabled, `nix develop` in the repo gives you a reproducible shell with CUDA 13, LLVM 22, Clang, and the pinned Rust nightly — no manual apt installs. The shellHook auto-discovers host NVIDIA drivers on NixOS and non-NixOS systems. ```bash nix develop # full dev shell in this repo nix run github:NVlabs/cuda-oxide#new my-project # bootstrap a project ``` #### Rust ```bash # Toolchain installed automatically via rust-toolchain.toml # Manual install if needed: rustup toolchain install nightly-2026-04-03 rustup component add rust-src rustc-dev --toolchain nightly-2026-04-03 ``` #### CUDA ```bash export PATH="/usr/local/cuda/bin:$PATH" nvcc --version ``` #### LLVM (optional) ```bash # Ubuntu/Debian sudo apt install llvm-21 ``` If your distro packages do not provide `llvm-21`, use LLVM's apt helper: ```bash sudo apt-get install -y lsb-release wget software-properties-common gnupg wget https://apt.llvm.org/llvm.sh && chmod +x llvm.sh sudo ./llvm.sh 21 ``` ```bash # Verify NVPTX support llc-21 --version | grep nvptx ``` The pipeline prefers `llc` in Rust toolchain, and auto-discovers `llc-22` and `llc-21` on `PATH` (in that order). To pin a specific binary, set `CUDA_OXIDE_LLC=/usr/bin/llc-21`. > We emit TMA / tcgen05 / WGMMA intrinsics that `llc` from LLVM 20 and earlier can't handle. > Simple kernels might still work with an older `llc`, but anything Hopper / Blackwell needs 21+. #### Clang (host `cuda-bindings`) The host `cuda-bindings` crate runs `bindgen`, which loads libclang and needs clang's own resource-dir `stddef.h` — a bare `libclang1-*` runtime is not enough. ```bash sudo apt install clang-21 # or libclang-common-21-dev ``` `cargo oxide doctor` catches this up front; the symptom otherwise is a cryptic `'stddef.h' file not found` during the host build. #### Dev Container The repository includes a standard devcontainer setup in `.devcontainer/` for a reproducible CUDA, LLVM, Clang, and Rust environment. See the [installation chapter](cuda-oxide-book/getting-started/installation.md#dev-container) for editor and CLI usage. ### Verifying Installation ```bash # Check that all prerequisites are in place cargo oxide doctor # Build and run an example end-to-end cargo oxide run vecadd ``` `cargo oxide doctor` validates your Rust toolchain, CUDA toolkit, LLVM, and codegen backend. If everything is configured correctly, `cargo oxide run vecadd` compiles a Rust kernel to PTX, launches it on the GPU, and prints `✓ SUCCESS: All 1024 elements correct!`. ## Examples **60+ examples** in `crates/rustc-codegen-cuda/examples/`. Highlights: | Example | Description | |----------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------| | `vecadd` | Vector addition -- canonical first example | | `host_closure` | Generic kernels with closures passed from host | | `generic` | Generic kernels with monomorphization (`scale`) | | `ord_cmp` | Device-side `Ord::cmp` lowering for signed and unsigned integers | | `gemm_sol_final` | Canonical Blackwell GEMM SoL: size-specialized CLC + cg2 + vector stores | | `gemm_sol` | Historical GEMM kernel progression and comparison kernels | | `tcgen05` | Blackwell tensor cores (sm_100a): TMEM, MMA, cta_group::2 | | `atomics` | GPU atomics: 6 types x 3 scopes x 5 orderings (20 tests) | | `atomic_f16` | Scalar f16 atomics: per-scope correctness checks + f32 vs f16 bench | | `cluster` | Thread Block Clusters + DSMEM ring exchange (Hopper+) | | `async_mlp` | Async MLP pipeline: GEMM → MatVec → ReLU across concurrent streams | | `mathdx_ffi_test` | cuFFTDx thread-level FFT + cuBLASDx block-level GEMM | | `device_ffi_test` | Device FFI: Rust kernels calling C++ CCCL warp-level reductions via LTOIR| | `async_vecadd` | Async GPU execution with `cuda-async` and `DeviceOperation` | | `cross_crate_kernel` | Library crates defining kernels, bundled into binaries | | `cuda_module_in_lib` | `#[cuda_module]` in a library crate, loaded by embedded bundle name | ```bash cargo oxide run vecadd cargo oxide run gemm_sol_final ``` ## Crate Overview ### User-Facing Crates | Crate | Description | |---------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------| | `cuda-device` | Device intrinsics (`thread::*`, `warp::*`, barriers) | | `cuda-host` | Typed module loading, launch helpers, LTOIR loader | | `cuda-macros` | Proc macros (`#[cuda_module]`, `#[kernel]`, `gpu_printf!`) | | `cuda-bindings` | Raw `bindgen` FFI bindings to `cuda.h` | | `cuda-core` | Safe RAII wrappers (`CudaContext`, `CudaStream`, `DeviceBuffer`, ...) | | `cuda-async` | Async execution layer (`DeviceOperation`, `DeviceFuture`, `DeviceBox`) | | `libnvvm-sys` | `dlopen` bindings to libNVVM (used by `cuda-host::ltoir`) | | `nvjitlink-sys` | `dlopen` bindings to nvJitLink (used by `cuda-host::ltoir`) | ### Compiler Crates | Crate | Description | |----------------------|-------------------------------------------------------| | `rustc-codegen-cuda` | Custom rustc backend | | `mir-importer` | Rust MIR -> `dialect-mir` translation + pipeline | | `mir-lower` | `dialect-mir` -> LLVM dialect lowering | | `dialect-mir` | pliron dialect modelling Rust MIR | | `llvm-export` | pliron-llvm shim + textual `.ll` exporter | | `dialect-nvvm` | pliron dialect modelling NVVM intrinsics | ### Build Tooling | Crate | Description | |----------------|------------------------------------------------------| | `cargo-oxide` | Cargo subcommand (`cargo oxide run`, etc.) | ### Documentation | Directory | Description | |---------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------| | `cuda-oxide-book` | Project book (Sphinx + MyST) — guides, compiler internals, API ref | ## Status ### Highlights: - End-to-end Rust -> PTX compilation - Unified single-source compilation (host + device in one file) - Generic functions with monomorphization - Closures with captures (move and non-move via HMM) - User-defined structs, enums, pattern matching - Full GPU intrinsic support (thread, warp, shared memory, barriers, TMA, clusters, atomics) - Cross-crate kernels - LTOIR generation for Blackwell+ (device-side LTO) - Device FFI: Rust <-> C++/CCCL interop via LTOIR - MathDx integration: cuFFTDx thread-level FFT, cuBLASDx block-level GEMM - Tile interop (experimental): [`cutile_inter_kernel`](crates/rustc-codegen-cuda/examples/cutile_inter_kernel/README.md) chains a cutile-rs Tile kernel and a cuda-oxide SIMT PTX kernel on the same CUDA stream over shared device tensors. Intra-kernel Tile interop is work in progress and tracked in [#96](https://github.com/NVlabs/cuda-oxide/issues/96). - Host runtime: `cuda-core` (explicit control, pinned host transfers) and `cuda-async` (composable async operations) - Canonical Blackwell GEMM SoL example with size-specialized M256xN256/M512xN256 CLC + cta_group::2 kernels and vectorized epilogues (see `gemm_sol_final`) ## Documentation **WIP:** 🚧 The **[cuda-oxide book](https://nvlabs.github.io/cuda-oxide/)** is the primary reference for the project. It covers SIMT kernel authoring in Rust, synchronous and asynchronous GPU programming, the compiler architecture, and more. To build and serve the book locally, see [cuda-oxide-book/README.md](./cuda-oxide-book/README.md). ## Ecosystem cuda-oxide is one of several Rust + GPU efforts under active development. Projects in this space address different parts of the problem — Vulkan/SPIR-V for graphics, implicit offload via LLVM, third-party CUDA backends, safe driver bindings — and we've been working with maintainers across the broader Rust GPU community on how to move GPU computing in Rust forward together. For where cuda-oxide fits relative to other projects, see the [Ecosystem appendix](https://nvlabs.github.io/cuda-oxide/appendix/ecosystem.html) of the book. ## License The `cuda-bindings` crate is licensed under the NVIDIA Software License: [LICENSE-NVIDIA](LICENSE-NVIDIA). All other crates are licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0: [LICENSE-APACHE](LICENSE-APACHE).

file.content:
[workspace]
resolver = "3"
members = [
    # Core crates
    "crates/cuda-device",
    "crates/cuda-host",
    "crates/cuda-macros",
    "crates/llvm-export",
    "crates/dialect-mir",
    "crates/dialect-nvvm",
    "crates/mir-importer",
    "crates/mir-lower",
    "crates/mir-transforms",
    "crates/nvvm-transforms",
    "crates/cargo-oxide",
    "crates/oxide-artifacts",
    # FFI bindings
    "crates/cuda-bindings",
    "crates/cuda-core",
    "crates/cuda-async",
    "crates/libnvvm-sys",
    "crates/nvjitlink-sys",
    # Internal naming contract (workspace-private; publish = false)
    "crates/reserved-oxide-symbols",
    # Differential codegen fuzzer support
    "crates/fuzzer",
]

[workspace.package]
edition = "2024"
authors = ["Nihal Pasham <nihalp@nvidia.com>"]
license = "Apache-2.0"
repository = "https://github.com/NVlabs/cuda-oxide.git"

# Note: crates/rustc-codegen-cuda is NOT a workspace member because it requires
# special rustc nightly features and a different build process.
# Build and run examples with: cargo oxide run <example>
# Examples are in: crates/rustc-codegen-cuda/examples/

[workspace.dependencies]
# Pliron MLIR framework, pinned to upstream master. The additions cuda-oxide
# needs (address spaces, atomics, inline asm, FP16, stronger verification) landed
# upstream in pliron via #94 (which absorbed #116), so we track master directly.
# pliron-llvm uses default-features = false to keep llvm-sys (LLVM 22) out:
# cuda-oxide consumes the dialect modeling only, not the FFI bridge.
# `crates/rustc-codegen-cuda/Cargo.toml` MUST stay on this same rev, or pliron
# resolves to two distinct crates and types stop unifying.
pliron = { git = "https://github.com/pliron-org/pliron", rev = "222dd962a124cba8ec5f119ae6e0ecf202630854" }
pliron-derive = { git = "https://github.com/pliron-org/pliron", rev = "222dd962a124cba8ec5f119ae6e0ecf202630854" }
pliron-llvm = { git = "https://github.com/pliron-org/pliron", rev = "222dd962a124cba8ec5f119ae6e0ecf202630854", default-features = false }

# Internal crates
llvm-export = { path = "crates/llvm-export" }
dialect-mir = { path = "crates/dialect-mir" }
dialect-nvvm = { path = "crates/dialect-nvvm" }
mir-lower = { path = "crates/mir-lower" }
mir-importer = { path = "crates/mir-importer" }
mir-transforms = { path = "crates/mir-transforms" }
nvvm-transforms = { path = "crates/nvvm-transforms" }
cuda-device = { path = "crates/cuda-device" }
cuda-host = { path = "crates/cuda-host" }
cuda-macros = { path = "crates/cuda-macros" }
cuda-bindings = { path = "crates/cuda-bindings" }
cuda-core = { path = "crates/cuda-core" }
cuda-async = { path = "crates/cuda-async" }
libnvvm-sys = { path = "crates/libnvvm-sys" }
nvjitlink-sys = { path = "crates/nvjitlink-sys" }
reserved-oxide-symbols = { path = "crates/reserved-oxide-symbols" }
fuzzer = { path = "crates/fuzzer" }
oxide-artifacts = { path = "crates/oxide-artifacts" }

# Common dependencies
futures = "0.3"
thiserror = "2"
linkme = "0.3"
anyhow = "1.0"
once_cell = "1.18"
combine = "4.6"
log = "0.4"
half = "2.7"
libloading = "0.8"
sha2 = "0.10"

# llvm-export deps
rustc-hash = "2.0"
bitflags = "2.0"
dyn-clone = "1.0"

# cuda-macros deps
quote = "1.0"
syn = { version = "2.0", features = ["full", "visit", "visit-mut"] }
proc-macro2 = { version = "1.0", features = ["span-locations"] }

1	/*
2	 * SPDX-FileCopyrightText: Copyright (c) 2026 NVIDIA CORPORATION & AFFILIATES. All rights reserved.
3	 * SPDX-License-Identifier: Apache-2.0
4	 */
5	
6	//! cargo-oxide: Cargo subcommand for building and running cuda-oxide programs.
7	//!
8	//! Replaces the xtask pattern with a proper cargo subcommand that works both
9	//! inside the cuda-oxide repo (for developers) and externally (for users).
10	//!
11	//! # Usage
12	//!
13	//! ```bash
14	//! cargo oxide run vecadd              # build + run an example
15	//! cargo oxide build vecadd            # build only
16	//! cargo oxide pipeline vecadd         # verbose pipeline dump
17	//! cargo oxide debug vecadd --tui      # build + cuda-gdb
18	//! cargo oxide new my_kernel            # scaffold a standalone project
19	//! cargo oxide new my_kernel --async   # scaffold with async template
20	//! cargo oxide fmt                     # format all crates
21	//! cargo oxide doctor                  # check environment
22	//! cargo oxide setup                   # explicitly build/install backend
23	//! ```
24	
25	use clap::{Parser, Subcommand};
26	use std::path::PathBuf;
27	
28	mod backend;
29	mod commands;
30	
31	/// Top-level CLI structure parsed by clap.
32	///
33	/// The binary is named `cargo-oxide` so that `cargo oxide <subcommand>` works
34	/// as a cargo subcommand. The workspace alias in `.cargo/config.toml` also
35	/// routes `cargo oxide` here when run inside the repo.
36	#[derive(Parser)]
37	#[command(
38	    name = "cargo-oxide",
39	    bin_name = "cargo oxide",
40	    about = "Build and run Rust GPU programs with cuda-oxide",
41	    version
42	)]
43	struct Cli {
44	    #[command(subcommand)]
45	    command: Commands,
46	}
47	
48	/// Available subcommands for `cargo oxide`.
49	#[derive(Subcommand)]
50	enum Commands {
51	    /// Build and run an example or project
52	    Run {
53	        /// Example name (required in workspace, optional for standalone projects)
54	        example: Option<String>,
55	        /// Generate NVVM IR (use with libNVVM -gen-lto)
56	        #[arg(long)]
57	        emit_nvvm_ir: bool,
58	        /// Target architecture (e.g., sm_90, sm_100, sm_120). When omitted,
59	        /// `run` auto-detects the compute capability of CUDA device 0 so the
60	        /// generated module loads on the local GPU; set `CUDA_OXIDE_TARGET`
61	        /// in the environment for a non-interactive override.
62	        #[arg(long)]
63	        arch: Option<String>,
64	        /// Comma-separated list of features to enable
65	        #[arg(long)]
66	        features: Option<String>,
67	        /// Pick a specific binary in a multi-bin package (forwarded as
68	        /// `cargo run --bin <name>`). Defaults to the package's
69	        /// `default-run`.
70	        #[arg(long)]
71	        bin: Option<String>,
72	        /// Show verbose compilation output
73	        #[arg(short, long)]
74	        verbose: bool,
75	        /// Disable FMA contraction (default: on, matching nvcc --fmad=true).
76	        /// Also settable via CUDA_OXIDE_NO_FMA=1.
77	        #[arg(long)]
78	        no_fmad: bool,
79	    },
80	    /// Build an example or project (compile only, don't run)
81	    Build {
82	        /// Example name (required in workspace, optional for standalone projects)
83	        example: Option<String>,
84	        /// Generate NVVM IR (use with libNVVM -gen-lto)
85	        #[arg(long)]
86	        emit_nvvm_ir: bool,
87	        /// Target architecture (e.g., sm_90, sm_100, sm_120)
88	        #[arg(long)]
89	        arch: Option<String>,
90	        /// Comma-separated list of features to enable
91	        #[arg(long)]
92	        features: Option<String>,
93	        /// Show verbose compilation output
94	        #[arg(short, long)]
95	        verbose: bool,
96	        /// Disable FMA contraction (default: on, matching nvcc --fmad=true).
97	        /// Also settable via CUDA_OXIDE_NO_FMA=1.
98	        #[arg(long)]
99	        no_fmad: bool,
100	        /// Cargo target directory for passthrough mode
101	        #[arg(long)]
102	        cargo_target_dir: Option<PathBuf>,
103	        /// Comma-separated cuda-oxide owner crate filter for device codegen
104	        #[arg(long)]
105	        device_codegen_crate: Option<String>,
106	        /// Repeatable cfg appended as `--cfg NAME` for passthrough device codegen
107	        #[arg(long = "device-cfg")]
108	        device_cfgs: Vec<String>,
109	        /// Cargo build arguments for passthrough mode. Use after `--`.
110	        #[arg(last = true, num_args = 0.., allow_hyphen_values = true)]
111	        cargo_args: Vec<String>,
112	    },
113	    /// Run Cargo tests through the cuda-oxide backend
114	    Test {
115	        /// Target architecture (e.g., sm_90, sm_100, sm_120)
116	        #[arg(long)]
117	        arch: Option<String>,
118	        /// Cargo target directory
119	        #[arg(long)]
120	        cargo_target_dir: Option<PathBuf>,
121	        /// Comma-separated cuda-oxide owner crate filter for device codegen
122	        #[arg(long)]
123	        device_codegen_crate: Option<String>,
124	        /// Repeatable cfg appended as `--cfg NAME` for device codegen
125	        #[arg(long = "device-cfg")]
126	        device_cfgs: Vec<String>,
127	        /// Show verbose compilation output
128	        #[arg(short, long)]
129	        verbose: bool,
130	        /// Cargo test arguments. Use after `--`; empty runs plain `cargo test`.
131	        #[arg(last = true, num_args = 0.., allow_hyphen_values = true)]
132	        cargo_args: Vec<String>,
133	    },
134	    /// Compile a crate's device code to a binary LTOIR artifact in one step.
135	    ///
136	    /// Produces the SIMT artifact a tile or C++ kernel links against
137	    /// (NVVM IR emission followed by libNVVM `-gen-lto`), writing
138	    /// `<crate>.ltoir`. See the Tile-to-SIMT interop tracker (#96).
139	    EmitLtoir {
140	        /// Crate name (required in workspace, optional for standalone projects)
141	        example: Option<String>,
142	        /// Target architecture (e.g. sm_90, sm_100, sm_120). Required: LTOIR is
143	        /// architecture-specific.
144	        #[arg(long)]
145	        arch: String,
146	        /// Comma-separated list of features to enable
147	        #[arg(long)]
148	        features: Option<String>,
149	        /// Output path for the `.ltoir` file (default: `<crate-dir>/<crate>.ltoir`)
150	        #[arg(long, short)]
151	        output: Option<PathBuf>,
152	        /// Show verbose compilation output
153	        #[arg(short, long)]
154	        verbose: bool,
155	    },
156	    /// Show the full compilation pipeline (MIR -> PTX/NVVM IR) with verbose output
157	    Pipeline {
158	        /// Example name (required in workspace, optional for standalone projects)
159	        example: Option<String>,
160	        /// Generate NVVM IR (use with libNVVM -gen-lto)
161	        #[arg(long)]
162	        emit_nvvm_ir: bool,
163	        /// Target architecture (e.g., sm_90, sm_100, sm_120)
164	        #[arg(long)]
165	        arch: Option<String>,
166	        /// Disable FMA contraction (default: on, matching nvcc --fmad=true).
167	        /// Also settable via CUDA_OXIDE_NO_FMA=1.
168	        #[arg(long)]
169	        no_fmad: bool,
170	    },
171	    /// Build with debug info and launch cuda-gdb
172	    Debug {
173	        /// Example name (required in workspace, optional for standalone projects)
174	        example: Option<String>,
175	        /// Target architecture (e.g., sm_90, sm_100, sm_120). When omitted,
176	        /// `debug` auto-detects the compute capability of CUDA device 0 so the
177	        /// generated module loads on the local GPU; set `CUDA_OXIDE_TARGET`
178	        /// in the environment for a non-interactive override.
179	        #[arg(long)]
180	        arch: Option<String>,
181	        /// Use cgdb frontend (better source view, vim keys)
182	        #[arg(long)]
183	        cgdb: bool,
184	        /// Use GDB's built-in TUI interface
185	        #[arg(long)]
186	        tui: bool,
187	    },
188	    /// Format all crates (root workspace, codegen backend, examples)
189	    Fmt {
190	        /// Check formatting without modifying files
191	        #[arg(long)]
192	        check: bool,
193	    },
194	    /// Scaffold a new standalone cuda-oxide project
195	    New {
196	        /// Project name (becomes directory name and package name)
197	        name: String,
198	        /// Use async template (tokio + cuda-async + DeviceOperation)
199	        #[arg(long = "async")]
200	        async_mode: bool,
201	    },
202	    /// Check that your environment is set up correctly
203	    Doctor,
204	    /// Build and cache the codegen backend
205	    Setup,
206	}
207	
208	fn has_passthrough_separator(args: &[String]) -> bool {
209	    args.iter().skip(2).any(|arg| arg == "--")
210	}
211	
212	fn use_build_passthrough(
213	    explicit_separator: bool,
214	    cargo_target_dir_is_set: bool,
215	    owner_filter_is_set: bool,
216	    has_device_cfgs: bool,
217	    has_cargo_args: bool,
218	) -> bool {
219	    explicit_separator
220	        || cargo_target_dir_is_set
221	        || owner_filter_is_set
222	        || has_device_cfgs
223	        || has_cargo_args
224	}
225	
226	fn main() {
227	    // Handle both invocation methods:
228	    // 1. Cargo subcommand: `cargo oxide run vecadd` → argv = ["cargo-oxide", "oxide", "run", "vecadd"]
229	    // 2. Cargo alias:      `cargo oxide run vecadd` → argv = ["target/.../cargo-oxide", "run", "vecadd"]
230	    let args: Vec<String> = std::env::args().collect();
231	    let effective_args = if args.get(1).map(|s| s.as_str()) == Some("oxide") {
232	        let mut filtered = vec![args[0].clone()];
233	        filtered.extend(args[2..].iter().cloned());
234	        filtered
235	    } else {
236	        args
237	    };
238	
239	    let explicit_passthrough = has_passthrough_separator(&effective_args);
240	    let cli = Cli::parse_from(effective_args);
241	
242	    match cli.command {
243	        Commands::Run {
244	            example,
245	            emit_nvvm_ir,
246	            arch,
247	            features,
248	            bin,
249	            verbose,
250	            no_fmad,
251	        } => {
252	            let ctx = commands::resolve_context();
253	            let example = resolve_example_name(example, &ctx, "run");
254	            validate_nvvm_ir_arch(&ctx, &example, emit_nvvm_ir, arch.as_deref());
255	            commands::codegen_run(
256	                &ctx,
257	                &example,
258	                verbose,
259	                emit_nvvm_ir,
260	                arch.as_deref(),
261	                features.as_deref(),
262	                bin.as_deref(),
263	                no_fmad,
264	            );
265	        }
266	        Commands::Build {
267	            example,
268	            emit_nvvm_ir,
269	            arch,
270	            features,
271	            verbose,
272	            no_fmad,
273	            cargo_target_dir,
274	            device_codegen_crate,
275	            device_cfgs,
276	            cargo_args,
277	        } => {
278	            let ctx = commands::resolve_context();
279	            let passthrough = use_build_passthrough(
280	                explicit_passthrough,
281	                cargo_target_dir.is_some(),
282	                device_codegen_crate.is_some(),
283	                !device_cfgs.is_empty(),
284	                !cargo_args.is_empty(),
285	            );
286	            if !passthrough {
287	                let example = resolve_example_name(example, &ctx, "build");
288	                validate_nvvm_ir_arch(&ctx, &example, emit_nvvm_ir, arch.as_deref());
289	                commands::codegen_build(
290	                    &ctx,
291	                    &example,
292	                    verbose,
293	                    emit_nvvm_ir,
294	                    arch.as_deref(),
295	                    features.as_deref(),
296	                    no_fmad,
297	                );
298	            } else {
299	                if example.is_some() {
300	                    eprintln!(
301	                        "Error: `cargo oxide build` accepts either an example name or passthrough args after `--`, not both"
302	                    );
303	                    std::process::exit(2);
304	                }
305	                validate_nvvm_ir_arch(&ctx, "cargo build", emit_nvvm_ir, arch.as_deref());
306	                commands::codegen_cargo_passthrough(
307	                    &ctx,
308	                    "build",
309	                    commands::CargoPassthroughOptions {
310	                        verbose,
311	                        emit_nvvm_ir,
312	                        arch: arch.as_deref(),
313	                        features: features.as_deref(),
314	                        cargo_target_dir: cargo_target_dir.as_deref(),
315	                        device_codegen_crate: device_codegen_crate.as_deref(),
316	                        device_cfgs: &device_cfgs,
317	                        no_fmad,
318	                    },
319	                    &cargo_args,
320	                );
321	            }
322	        }
323	        Commands::Test {
324	            arch,
325	            cargo_target_dir,
326	            device_codegen_crate,
327	            device_cfgs,
328	            verbose,
329	            cargo_args,
330	        } => {
331	            let ctx = commands::resolve_context();
332	            commands::codegen_cargo_passthrough(
333	                &ctx,
334	                "test",
335	                commands::CargoPassthroughOptions {
336	                    verbose,
337	                    emit_nvvm_ir: false,
338	                    arch: arch.as_deref(),
339	                    features: None,
340	                    cargo_target_dir: cargo_target_dir.as_deref(),
341	                    device_codegen_crate: device_codegen_crate.as_deref(),
342	                    device_cfgs: &device_cfgs,
343	                    no_fmad: false,
344	                },
345	                &cargo_args,
346	            );
347	        }
348	        Commands::EmitLtoir {
349	            example,
350	            arch,
351	            features,
352	            output,
353	            verbose,
354	        } => {
355	            let ctx = commands::resolve_context();
356	            let example = resolve_example_name(example, &ctx, "emit-ltoir");
357	            commands::emit_ltoir(
358	                &ctx,
359	                &example,

364	            );
365	        }
366	        Commands::Pipeline {
367	            example,
368	            emit_nvvm_ir,
369	            arch,
370	            no_fmad,
371	        } => {
372	            let ctx = commands::resolve_context();
373	            let example = resolve_example_name(example, &ctx, "pipeline");
374	            validate_nvvm_ir_arch(&ctx, &example, emit_nvvm_ir, arch.as_deref());
375	            commands::codegen_show_pipeline(&ctx, &example, emit_nvvm_ir, arch.as_deref(), no_fmad);
376	        }
377	        Commands::Debug {
378	            example,
379	            arch,
380	            cgdb,
381	            tui,
382	        } => {
383	            let ctx = commands::resolve_context();
384	            let example = resolve_example_name(example, &ctx, "debug");
385	            commands::codegen_debug(&ctx, &example, arch.as_deref(), cgdb, tui);
386	        }
387	        Commands::Fmt { check } => {
388	            let ctx = commands::resolve_context();
389	            commands::format_all(&ctx, check);
390	        }
391	        Commands::New { name, async_mode } => {
392	            commands::scaffold_new(&name, async_mode);
393	        }
394	        Commands::Doctor => {
395	            // Side-effect-free resolver: doctor must never build the backend
396	            // (or clone anything) before it can diagnose the environment.
397	            let ctx = commands::resolve_doctor_context();
398	            commands::doctor(&ctx);
399	        }
400	        Commands::Setup => {
401	            let ctx = commands::resolve_context();
402	            commands::setup(&ctx);
403	        }
404	    }
405	}
406	
407	/// Resolves the example/project name from the CLI argument or context.
408	///
409	/// In workspace mode the name is required; in standalone mode it defaults
410	/// to the current directory name (which matches the package name from
411	/// `cargo oxide new`).
412	fn resolve_example_name(name: Option<String>, ctx: &commands::Context, subcommand: &str) -> String {
413	    if let Some(n) = name {
414	        return n;
415	    }
416	    if !ctx.is_workspace {
417	        return std::env::current_dir()
418	            .ok()
419	            .and_then(|p| p.file_name().map(|n| n.to_string_lossy().into_owned()))
420	            .unwrap_or_else(|| {
421	                eprintln!("Error: could not determine project name from current directory");
422	                std::process::exit(1);
423	            });
424	    }
425	    eprintln!("Error: <EXAMPLE> is required when running inside the cuda-oxide workspace.");
426	    eprintln!();
427	    eprintln!("Usage: cargo oxide {subcommand} <EXAMPLE>");
428	    eprintln!();
429	    eprintln!("Available examples are in crates/rustc-codegen-cuda/examples/");
430	    std::process::exit(1);
431	}
432	
433	/// Ensures an architecture is configured when `--emit-nvvm-ir` is used.
434	///
435	/// NVVM IR output is architecture-specific, so omitting every target source
436	/// would produce an unusable artifact. Exits with a descriptive error.
437	fn validate_nvvm_ir_arch(
438	    ctx: &commands::Context,
439	    example: &str,
440	    emit_nvvm_ir: bool,
441	    arch: Option<&str>,
442	) {
443	    if emit_nvvm_ir && !commands::has_configured_arch(ctx, arch) {
444	        eprintln!("Error: --emit-nvvm-ir requires a target architecture");
445	        eprintln!();
446	        eprintln!("NVVM IR output is architecture-specific. Pass --arch, set");
447	        eprintln!("CUDA_OXIDE_TARGET, or configure default-arch. For example:");
448	        eprintln!("  --arch sm_120    Blackwell (RTX 50 series)");
449	        eprintln!("  --arch sm_100    Blackwell");
450	        eprintln!();
451	        eprintln!("Example:");
452	        eprintln!("  cargo oxide run {} --emit-nvvm-ir --arch sm_120", example);
453	        std::process::exit(1);
454	    }
455	}
456	
457	#[cfg(test)]
458	mod tests {
459	    use super::*;
460	
461	    fn strings(args: &[&str]) -> Vec<String> {
462	        args.iter().map(|arg| (*arg).to_string()).collect()
463	    }
464	
465	    #[test]
466	    fn build_parser_preserves_nested_cargo_and_test_separators() {
467	        let args = strings(&[
468	            "cargo-oxide",
469	            "build",
470	            "--cargo-target-dir",
471	            "target/cuda",
472	            "--",
473	            "-p",
474	            "gpu-app",
475	            "--test",
476	            "smoke",
477	            "--",
478	            "--nocapture",
479	        ]);
480	        assert!(has_passthrough_separator(&args));
481	
482	        let cli = Cli::try_parse_from(args).expect("passthrough CLI should parse");
483	        let Commands::Build {
484	            cargo_target_dir,
485	            cargo_args,
486	            ..
487	        } = cli.command
488	        else {
489	            panic!("expected build command");
490	        };
491	        assert_eq!(cargo_target_dir, Some(PathBuf::from("target/cuda")));
492	        assert_eq!(
493	            cargo_args,
494	            strings(&["-p", "gpu-app", "--test", "smoke", "--", "--nocapture"])
495	        );
496	    }
497	
498	    #[test]
499	    fn empty_test_and_explicit_empty_build_passthrough_are_distinct() {
500	        let test_cli = Cli::try_parse_from(["cargo-oxide", "test"])
501	            .expect("cargo oxide test should accept no Cargo arguments");
502	        let Commands::Test { cargo_args, .. } = test_cli.command else {
503	            panic!("expected test command");
504	        };
505	        assert!(cargo_args.is_empty());
506	
507	        let build_args = strings(&["cargo-oxide", "build", "--"]);
508	        assert!(has_passthrough_separator(&build_args));
509	        let build_cli = Cli::try_parse_from(build_args).expect("empty passthrough should parse");
510	        let Commands::Build { cargo_args, .. } = build_cli.command else {
511	            panic!("expected build command");
512	        };
513	        assert!(cargo_args.is_empty());
514	    }
515	
516	    #[test]
517	    fn build_mode_uses_only_unambiguous_passthrough_signals() {
518	        assert!(!use_build_passthrough(false, false, false, false, false));
519	        assert!(use_build_passthrough(true, false, false, false, false));
520	        assert!(use_build_passthrough(false, true, false, false, false));
521	        assert!(use_build_passthrough(false, false, true, false, false));
522	        assert!(use_build_passthrough(false, false, false, true, false));
523	        assert!(use_build_passthrough(false, false, false, false, true));
524	    }
525	}

1 # cargo-oxide 2 3 Cargo subcommand for building and running Rust GPU programs with cuda-oxide. 4 5 Replaces the previous `xtask` pattern with a proper cargo subcommand that works both inside the cuda-oxide repo (for developers) and externally (for users who `cargo install`). 6 7 ## Installation 8 9 **Internal developers** (inside the cuda-oxide repo): no installation needed. The workspace alias makes `cargo oxide` work immediately. 10 11 **External users**: 12 13 Install with the project's pinned nightly toolchain: 14 15 ```bash 16 cargo +nightly-2026-04-03 install --git https://github.com/NVlabs/cuda-oxide.git cargo-oxide 17 ``` 18 19 On first run, `cargo-oxide` will automatically fetch and build the codegen backend if it's not already available. 20 21 ## Usage 22 23 ```bash 24 cargo oxide new my_project # scaffold a new cuda-oxide project 25 cargo oxide new my_project --async # scaffold with async template (tokio + cuda-async) 26 cargo oxide run vecadd # build + run an example 27 cargo oxide build vecadd # compile only (no run) 28 cargo oxide emit-ltoir vecadd --arch sm_100 # device code -> .ltoir (Tile/SIMT interop) 29 cargo oxide build -- -p my_app # arbitrary cargo build through cuda-oxide 30 cargo oxide test # cargo test with cuda-oxide defaults 31 cargo oxide test -- -p my_app # arbitrary cargo test through cuda-oxide 32 cargo oxide pipeline vecadd # verbose pipeline dump 33 # (MIR -> dialect-mir -> LLVM dialect -> LLVM IR -> PTX) 34 cargo oxide debug vecadd --tui # build + launch cuda-gdb 35 cargo oxide fmt # format all crates 36 cargo oxide fmt --check # check formatting 37 cargo oxide doctor # validate environment 38 cargo oxide setup # explicitly build the codegen backend 39 ``` 40 41 ### Flags 42 43 | Flag | Applies to | Description | 44 |------------------------------|----------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------| 45 | `--emit-nvvm-ir` | run, build, pipeline | Generate NVVM IR for libNVVM | 46 | `--arch ` | run, build, test, pipeline, emit-ltoir | Target architecture override | 47 | `--features ` | run, build, build passthrough, emit-ltoir | Comma-separated cargo features to enable | 48 | `-o, --output

` | emit-ltoir | Output path for the `.ltoir` artifact | 49 | `--cargo-target-dir ` | build/test passthrough | Cargo target directory | 50 | `--device-codegen-crate ` | build/test passthrough | Comma-separated device owner crate filter | 51 | `--device-cfg ` | build/test passthrough | Append `--cfg NAME` to rustflags | 52 | `-v, --verbose` | run, build, test, emit-ltoir | Show detailed compilation output | 53 | `--no-fmad` | run, build, pipeline | Disable FMA contraction (keep separate mul+add) | 54 | `--async` | new | Use the async template | 55 | `--cgdb` | debug | Use cgdb instead of cuda-gdb | 56 | `--tui` | debug | Use GDB's TUI interface | 57 | `--check` | fmt | Check formatting only | 58 59 `--arch` is required for `emit-ltoir` and explicit NVVM IR output because those 60 artifacts are architecture-specific. Without an override, `run` detects the 61 local GPU, while `build` and `pipeline` use the compiler's feature-based target 62 so they remain useful for cross-compilation. 63 64 `--no-fmad` disables FMA contraction for kernels that rely on two separate 65 roundings (e.g. Dekker's algorithm, 2Sum). Equivalent to `CUDA_OXIDE_NO_FMA=1`. 66 By default, FMA contraction is on (matching nvcc's `--fmad=true`): `fmul+fadd` 67 pairs fuse into a single `fma.rn.f32` for fewer instructions and one rounding. 68 69 ## Commands 70 71 ### `cargo oxide run ` 72 73 Builds the codegen backend, compiles the example with the custom backend, and runs it. This is the primary command for day-to-day development. 74 75 When neither `--arch` nor `CUDA_OXIDE_TARGET` is set, `run` detects the 76 compute capability of CUDA device 0 and targets that architecture so the 77 generated PTX can load on the local GPU. Use `--arch ` or 78 `CUDA_OXIDE_TARGET=` to override this for a specific device or 79 cross-target workflow. 80 81 ```bash 82 cargo oxide run vecadd 83 cargo oxide run gemm_sol 84 cargo oxide run device_ffi_test --emit-nvvm-ir --arch sm_120 85 cargo oxide run cutile_inter_kernel 86 ``` 87 88 Interop examples can declare extra cuda-oxide device crates with 89 `[[package.metadata.cuda-oxide.device-crates]]`, plus optional 90 `[package.metadata.cuda-oxide.interop]` metadata. `cargo oxide run` builds those 91 device crates with `rustc-codegen-cuda`, writes their PTX to the 92 configured location, and then builds/runs the host crate normally. 93 `cutile_inter_kernel` uses this path: 94 the host crate is a cutile-rs program, while `simt/` is a cuda-oxide SIMT PTX 95 crate loaded by the host at runtime. 96 97 ### `cargo oxide build ` 98 99 Same as `run` but stops after compilation. Useful for examples that require hardware you don't have (e.g., Blackwell tensor cores). 100 101 ```bash 102 cargo oxide build htens # compiles PTX, doesn't try to run on GPU 103 cargo oxide build tcgen05 # sm_100a only, but PTX generation works anywhere 104 ``` 105 106 `build` also has a passthrough mode for normal Cargo workspaces. Put the Cargo 107 arguments after `--`; cargo-oxide supplies the backend, target architecture, 108 configured environment, and optional device owner filters. 109 110 ```bash 111 cargo oxide build -- # plain `cargo build` 112 cargo oxide build --arch sm_86 -- -p my_app --bin app --release 113 cargo oxide build --cargo-target-dir target/cuda -- -p my_app --release 114 cargo oxide build --device-codegen-crate gpu-kernels,math_gpu -- -p my_app 115 ``` 116 117 The owner filter is matched against rustc crate names, so Cargo package hyphens 118 are normalized to underscores (`gpu-kernels` matches `gpu_kernels`). The CUDA 119 backend still compiles host code for every crate through LLVM; it emits device 120 artifacts only for the listed owner crates. 121 122 The filter controls compilation, not runtime module names. Code in an excluded 123 crate or target must not call that target's generated `load()` function. In a 124 package with several targets, embedded bundles still use the package name, so 125 an excluded target's loader could otherwise find a selected sibling target's 126 bundle. 127 128 Passthrough builds include the effective codegen settings and backend build in 129 Cargo's rustc fingerprint. Changing the target, output mode, FMA policy, owner 130 list, configured codegen environment, or backend `.so` therefore regenerates 131 device artifacts instead of reusing a stale Cargo result. 132 133 ### `cargo oxide emit-ltoir ` 134 135 Compiles a crate's device code to a binary LTOIR artifact in one step, for the 136 Tile-to-SIMT interop workflow ([#96](https://github.com/NVlabs/cuda-oxide/issues/96)): 137 cuda-oxide is the SIMT participant, producing LTOIR that a tile or CUDA C++ kernel 138 links against. It builds the crate in NVVM IR mode, then runs the emitted 139 `.ll` through libNVVM `-gen-lto`, writing `.ltoir`. 140 141 `--arch` is required, since LTOIR is architecture-specific. It accepts `sm_XX`, 142 `compute_XX`, or a bare `XX`. The default output path is `/.ltoir`; 143 `-o/--output` overrides it. 144 145 ```bash 146 cargo oxide emit-ltoir standalone_device_fn --arch sm_100 147 cargo oxide emit-ltoir my_simt_crate --arch sm_120 -o build/simt.ltoir 148 ``` 149 150 cuda-oxide chooses the NVVM IR format from the selected architecture. 151 Pre-Blackwell targets use LLVM 7 typed pointers; `compute_100` and newer targets 152 use modern opaque pointers. The target is checked before export and recorded 153 alongside the artifact so the runtime uses the same format. 154 155 ### `cargo oxide test` 156 157 Runs `cargo test` through the cuda-oxide backend. With no extra arguments it 158 runs the workspace's normal test selection. Put Cargo test arguments after 159 `--`, including the test-binary separator when needed. 160 161 ```bash 162 cargo oxide test 163 cargo oxide test -- -p my_app --release --test gpu_smoke -- --nocapture 164 cargo oxide test --device-codegen-crate gpu_smoke -- -p my_app --test gpu_smoke 165 ``` 166 167 ### `cargo oxide pipeline ` 168 169 Shows verbose progress plus the selected IR artifacts: MIR collection, 170 `dialect-mir` before and after `mem2reg`, LLVM dialect, LLVM IR, and final PTX. 171 172 ```bash 173 cargo oxide pipeline vecadd 174 cargo oxide pipeline device_ffi_test --emit-nvvm-ir --arch sm_120 175 ``` 176 177 ### `cargo oxide debug ` 178 179 Builds with debug info (`-C debuginfo=2`) and launches cuda-gdb. Supports `--tui` for GDB's TUI mode and `--cgdb` for the cgdb frontend. 180 181 ### `cargo oxide new [--async]` 182 183 Scaffolds a new standalone cuda-oxide project with `Cargo.toml`, `rust-toolchain.toml`, and a working `src/main.rs` containing a vector addition kernel. The default template uses `#[cuda_module]` with typed synchronous launch methods; `--async` generates a template with `tokio`, `cuda-async`, and typed lazy `DeviceOperation` launches. 184 185 ```bash 186 cargo oxide new my_kernel 187 cd my_kernel 188 cargo oxide run 189 ``` 190 191 ### `cargo oxide fmt [--check]` 192 193 Formats all crates in the workspace: root workspace, `rustc-codegen-cuda`, and all examples. With `--check`, reports files that need formatting without modifying them. 194 195 ### `cargo oxide doctor` 196 197 Validates that your environment is correctly set up: Rust nightly toolchain, 198 CUDA headers (`cuda.h`), CUDA toolkit (`nvcc`, libNVVM, nvJitLink, 199 libdevice), LLVM (`llc`), clang/libclang, the NVIDIA driver / GPU, and the 200 codegen backend `.so`. Every check reports what was found or how to fix it. 201 202 `cargo-oxide` itself builds and runs without the CUDA toolkit and without an 203 NVIDIA driver, and `doctor` never builds anything first, so it works on a 204 bare machine and tells you exactly what is missing. The driver / GPU check is 205 informational (only `cargo oxide run` needs a GPU), and a missing backend 206 `.so` just points at `cargo oxide setup` (`run`/`build` build it on demand 207 anyway). 208 209 ### `cargo oxide setup` 210 211 Explicitly builds (or rebuilds) the codegen backend. Normally this happens automatically on every `run`/`build`/`pipeline` command, but `setup` is useful after pulling new changes or for CI. 212 213 ## Backend Discovery 214 215 When `cargo oxide` needs the `librustc_codegen_cuda.so` backend, it searches in this order: 216 217 1. **`CUDA_OXIDE_BACKEND` env var** — explicit path override 218 2. **Project config** — `.cargo/cuda-oxide.toml` 219 3. **Local repo** — detects `crates/rustc-codegen-cuda` relative to workspace root, builds from source 220 4. **Cached `.so`** — checks `~/.cargo/cuda-oxide/librustc_codegen_cuda.so` 221 5. **Auto-fetch** — clones the cuda-oxide repo, builds, and caches (one-time) 222 223 Project config can also provide the default architecture, extra rustc flags, 224 and child-process environment: 225 226 ```toml 227 backend = "/path/to/librustc_codegen_cuda.so" 228 default-arch = "sm_86" 229 extra-rustflags = ["--cfg", "my_device_cfg"] 230 231 [env] 232 MY_BUILD_FLAG = "1" 233 ``` 234 235 Relative backend paths are resolved from the `.cargo` directory containing the 236 config file. Each `extra-rustflags` array element remains one rustc argument, 237 including values that contain spaces. 238 239 Configuration values are defaults. Precedence is: 240 241 1. explicit `cargo oxide` flags and internal artifact paths; 242 2. environment variables inherited by `cargo oxide`; 243 3. `.cargo/cuda-oxide.toml` defaults. 244 245 Do not put `RUSTFLAGS` or `CARGO_ENCODED_RUSTFLAGS` in the `[env]` table; use 246 `extra-rustflags` for project defaults. cargo-oxide combines configured flags, 247 inherited user flags, explicit `--device-cfg` values, and its required compiler 248 flags in boundary-preserving `CARGO_ENCODED_RUSTFLAGS`. Required compiler flags 249 are applied last so inherited settings cannot replace the cuda-oxide backend or 250 disable its correctness-critical codegen options. 251 252 ## Architecture 253 254 ```text 255 crates/cargo-oxide/ 256 ├── Cargo.toml 257 └── src/ 258 ├── main.rs # CLI definitions (clap) + dispatch 259 ├── backend.rs # Backend discovery + build logic 260 └── commands.rs # All command implementations 261 ``` 262 263 ## Future Commands 264 265 | Command | Description | 266 |---------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------| 267 | `cargo oxide bench ` | GPU profiling (nsys/ncu integration), report TFLOPS | 268 | `cargo oxide clean` | Remove generated PTX/LL/LTOIR artifacts and build caches| 269 | `cargo oxide update` | Update the cached codegen backend to latest version | 270 | `cargo oxide list` | List examples with descriptions and hardware reqs | 271 | `cargo oxide inspect ` | Show generated PTX without the full pipeline dump | 272

# cargo-oxide Cargo subcommand for building and running Rust GPU programs with cuda-oxide. Replaces the previous `xtask` pattern with a proper cargo subcommand that works both inside the cuda-oxide repo (for developers) and externally (for users who `cargo install`). ## Installation **Internal developers** (inside the cuda-oxide repo): no installation needed. The workspace alias makes `cargo oxide` work immediately. **External users**: Install with the project's pinned nightly toolchain: ```bash cargo +nightly-2026-04-03 install --git https://github.com/NVlabs/cuda-oxide.git cargo-oxide ``` On first run, `cargo-oxide` will automatically fetch and build the codegen backend if it's not already available. ## Usage ```bash cargo oxide new my_project # scaffold a new cuda-oxide project cargo oxide new my_project --async # scaffold with async template (tokio + cuda-async) cargo oxide run vecadd # build + run an example cargo oxide build vecadd # compile only (no run) cargo oxide emit-ltoir vecadd --arch sm_100 # device code -> .ltoir (Tile/SIMT interop) cargo oxide build -- -p my_app # arbitrary cargo build through cuda-oxide cargo oxide test # cargo test with cuda-oxide defaults cargo oxide test -- -p my_app # arbitrary cargo test through cuda-oxide cargo oxide pipeline vecadd # verbose pipeline dump # (MIR -> dialect-mir -> LLVM dialect -> LLVM IR -> PTX) cargo oxide debug vecadd --tui # build + launch cuda-gdb cargo oxide fmt # format all crates cargo oxide fmt --check # check formatting cargo oxide doctor # validate environment cargo oxide setup # explicitly build the codegen backend ``` ### Flags | Flag | Applies to | Description | |------------------------------|----------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------| | `--emit-nvvm-ir` | run, build, pipeline | Generate NVVM IR for libNVVM | | `--arch ` | run, build, test, pipeline, emit-ltoir | Target architecture override | | `--features ` | run, build, build passthrough, emit-ltoir | Comma-separated cargo features to enable | | `-o, --output

` | emit-ltoir | Output path for the `.ltoir` artifact | | `--cargo-target-dir ` | build/test passthrough | Cargo target directory | | `--device-codegen-crate ` | build/test passthrough | Comma-separated device owner crate filter | | `--device-cfg ` | build/test passthrough | Append `--cfg NAME` to rustflags | | `-v, --verbose` | run, build, test, emit-ltoir | Show detailed compilation output | | `--no-fmad` | run, build, pipeline | Disable FMA contraction (keep separate mul+add) | | `--async` | new | Use the async template | | `--cgdb` | debug | Use cgdb instead of cuda-gdb | | `--tui` | debug | Use GDB's TUI interface | | `--check` | fmt | Check formatting only | `--arch` is required for `emit-ltoir` and explicit NVVM IR output because those artifacts are architecture-specific. Without an override, `run` detects the local GPU, while `build` and `pipeline` use the compiler's feature-based target so they remain useful for cross-compilation. `--no-fmad` disables FMA contraction for kernels that rely on two separate roundings (e.g. Dekker's algorithm, 2Sum). Equivalent to `CUDA_OXIDE_NO_FMA=1`. By default, FMA contraction is on (matching nvcc's `--fmad=true`): `fmul+fadd` pairs fuse into a single `fma.rn.f32` for fewer instructions and one rounding. ## Commands ### `cargo oxide run ` Builds the codegen backend, compiles the example with the custom backend, and runs it. This is the primary command for day-to-day development. When neither `--arch` nor `CUDA_OXIDE_TARGET` is set, `run` detects the compute capability of CUDA device 0 and targets that architecture so the generated PTX can load on the local GPU. Use `--arch ` or `CUDA_OXIDE_TARGET=` to override this for a specific device or cross-target workflow. ```bash cargo oxide run vecadd cargo oxide run gemm_sol cargo oxide run device_ffi_test --emit-nvvm-ir --arch sm_120 cargo oxide run cutile_inter_kernel ``` Interop examples can declare extra cuda-oxide device crates with `[[package.metadata.cuda-oxide.device-crates]]`, plus optional `[package.metadata.cuda-oxide.interop]` metadata. `cargo oxide run` builds those device crates with `rustc-codegen-cuda`, writes their PTX to the configured location, and then builds/runs the host crate normally. `cutile_inter_kernel` uses this path: the host crate is a cutile-rs program, while `simt/` is a cuda-oxide SIMT PTX crate loaded by the host at runtime. ### `cargo oxide build ` Same as `run` but stops after compilation. Useful for examples that require hardware you don't have (e.g., Blackwell tensor cores). ```bash cargo oxide build htens # compiles PTX, doesn't try to run on GPU cargo oxide build tcgen05 # sm_100a only, but PTX generation works anywhere ``` `build` also has a passthrough mode for normal Cargo workspaces. Put the Cargo arguments after `--`; cargo-oxide supplies the backend, target architecture, configured environment, and optional device owner filters. ```bash cargo oxide build -- # plain `cargo build` cargo oxide build --arch sm_86 -- -p my_app --bin app --release cargo oxide build --cargo-target-dir target/cuda -- -p my_app --release cargo oxide build --device-codegen-crate gpu-kernels,math_gpu -- -p my_app ``` The owner filter is matched against rustc crate names, so Cargo package hyphens are normalized to underscores (`gpu-kernels` matches `gpu_kernels`). The CUDA backend still compiles host code for every crate through LLVM; it emits device artifacts only for the listed owner crates. The filter controls compilation, not runtime module names. Code in an excluded crate or target must not call that target's generated `load()` function. In a package with several targets, embedded bundles still use the package name, so an excluded target's loader could otherwise find a selected sibling target's bundle. Passthrough builds include the effective codegen settings and backend build in Cargo's rustc fingerprint. Changing the target, output mode, FMA policy, owner list, configured codegen environment, or backend `.so` therefore regenerates device artifacts instead of reusing a stale Cargo result. ### `cargo oxide emit-ltoir ` Compiles a crate's device code to a binary LTOIR artifact in one step, for the Tile-to-SIMT interop workflow ([#96](https://github.com/NVlabs/cuda-oxide/issues/96)): cuda-oxide is the SIMT participant, producing LTOIR that a tile or CUDA C++ kernel links against. It builds the crate in NVVM IR mode, then runs the emitted `.ll` through libNVVM `-gen-lto`, writing `.ltoir`. `--arch` is required, since LTOIR is architecture-specific. It accepts `sm_XX`, `compute_XX`, or a bare `XX`. The default output path is `/.ltoir`; `-o/--output` overrides it. ```bash cargo oxide emit-ltoir standalone_device_fn --arch sm_100 cargo oxide emit-ltoir my_simt_crate --arch sm_120 -o build/simt.ltoir ``` cuda-oxide chooses the NVVM IR format from the selected architecture. Pre-Blackwell targets use LLVM 7 typed pointers; `compute_100` and newer targets use modern opaque pointers. The target is checked before export and recorded alongside the artifact so the runtime uses the same format. ### `cargo oxide test` Runs `cargo test` through the cuda-oxide backend. With no extra arguments it runs the workspace's normal test selection. Put Cargo test arguments after `--`, including the test-binary separator when needed. ```bash cargo oxide test cargo oxide test -- -p my_app --release --test gpu_smoke -- --nocapture cargo oxide test --device-codegen-crate gpu_smoke -- -p my_app --test gpu_smoke ``` ### `cargo oxide pipeline ` Shows verbose progress plus the selected IR artifacts: MIR collection, `dialect-mir` before and after `mem2reg`, LLVM dialect, LLVM IR, and final PTX. ```bash cargo oxide pipeline vecadd cargo oxide pipeline device_ffi_test --emit-nvvm-ir --arch sm_120 ``` ### `cargo oxide debug ` Builds with debug info (`-C debuginfo=2`) and launches cuda-gdb. Supports `--tui` for GDB's TUI mode and `--cgdb` for the cgdb frontend. ### `cargo oxide new [--async]` Scaffolds a new standalone cuda-oxide project with `Cargo.toml`, `rust-toolchain.toml`, and a working `src/main.rs` containing a vector addition kernel. The default template uses `#[cuda_module]` with typed synchronous launch methods; `--async` generates a template with `tokio`, `cuda-async`, and typed lazy `DeviceOperation` launches. ```bash cargo oxide new my_kernel cd my_kernel cargo oxide run ``` ### `cargo oxide fmt [--check]` Formats all crates in the workspace: root workspace, `rustc-codegen-cuda`, and all examples. With `--check`, reports files that need formatting without modifying them. ### `cargo oxide doctor` Validates that your environment is correctly set up: Rust nightly toolchain, CUDA headers (`cuda.h`), CUDA toolkit (`nvcc`, libNVVM, nvJitLink, libdevice), LLVM (`llc`), clang/libclang, the NVIDIA driver / GPU, and the codegen backend `.so`. Every check reports what was found or how to fix it. `cargo-oxide` itself builds and runs without the CUDA toolkit and without an NVIDIA driver, and `doctor` never builds anything first, so it works on a bare machine and tells you exactly what is missing. The driver / GPU check is informational (only `cargo oxide run` needs a GPU), and a missing backend `.so` just points at `cargo oxide setup` (`run`/`build` build it on demand anyway). ### `cargo oxide setup` Explicitly builds (or rebuilds) the codegen backend. Normally this happens automatically on every `run`/`build`/`pipeline` command, but `setup` is useful after pulling new changes or for CI. ## Backend Discovery When `cargo oxide` needs the `librustc_codegen_cuda.so` backend, it searches in this order: 1. **`CUDA_OXIDE_BACKEND` env var** — explicit path override 2. **Project config** — `.cargo/cuda-oxide.toml` 3. **Local repo** — detects `crates/rustc-codegen-cuda` relative to workspace root, builds from source 4. **Cached `.so`** — checks `~/.cargo/cuda-oxide/librustc_codegen_cuda.so` 5. **Auto-fetch** — clones the cuda-oxide repo, builds, and caches (one-time) Project config can also provide the default architecture, extra rustc flags, and child-process environment: ```toml backend = "/path/to/librustc_codegen_cuda.so" default-arch = "sm_86" extra-rustflags = ["--cfg", "my_device_cfg"] [env] MY_BUILD_FLAG = "1" ``` Relative backend paths are resolved from the `.cargo` directory containing the config file. Each `extra-rustflags` array element remains one rustc argument, including values that contain spaces. Configuration values are defaults. Precedence is: 1. explicit `cargo oxide` flags and internal artifact paths; 2. environment variables inherited by `cargo oxide`; 3. `.cargo/cuda-oxide.toml` defaults. Do not put `RUSTFLAGS` or `CARGO_ENCODED_RUSTFLAGS` in the `[env]` table; use `extra-rustflags` for project defaults. cargo-oxide combines configured flags, inherited user flags, explicit `--device-cfg` values, and its required compiler flags in boundary-preserving `CARGO_ENCODED_RUSTFLAGS`. Required compiler flags are applied last so inherited settings cannot replace the cuda-oxide backend or disable its correctness-critical codegen options. ## Architecture ```text crates/cargo-oxide/ ├── Cargo.toml └── src/ ├── main.rs # CLI definitions (clap) + dispatch ├── backend.rs # Backend discovery + build logic └── commands.rs # All command implementations ``` ## Future Commands | Command | Description | |---------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------| | `cargo oxide bench ` | GPU profiling (nsys/ncu integration), report TFLOPS | | `cargo oxide clean` | Remove generated PTX/LL/LTOIR artifacts and build caches| | `cargo oxide update` | Update the cached codegen backend to latest version | | `cargo oxide list` | List examples with descriptions and hardware reqs | | `cargo oxide inspect ` | Show generated PTX without the full pipeline dump |
